content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA containing NNN integers, and an integer KKK (1≤K≤N1 \leq K \leq N1≤K≤N).

Find the number of subarrays of AAA with length KKK whose bitwise OR is odd.

**Note:** A subarray of AAA is a contiguous segment of elements of AAA.

For example, if A=[1,3,2]A = [1, 3, 2]A=[1,3,2], then it has 666 non-empty subarrays: [1],[3],[2],[1,3],[3,2],[1,3,2][1], [3], [2], [1, 3], [3, 2], [1, 3, 2][1],[3],[2],[1,3],[3,2],[1,3,2].

In particular, [1,2][1, 2][1,2] is not a subarray of AAA.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the length of the array and the subarray size you have to check, respectively.
The second line of each test contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the elements of the array.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of length-KKK subarrays of AAA whose bitwise OR is odd.
constraints: 1 <= N <= 500000 (sum of N over all test cases <= 500000)
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two space‑separated integers N and K — the length of the array and the required subarray size. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of subarrays of length K whose bitwise OR is odd.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case windows of size 2: [5,7] OR=7 (odd), [7,13] OR=15 (odd), [13,4] OR=13 (odd), [4,6] OR=6 (even) → 3 odd windows. Second test case windows of size 3: [2,6,7] OR=7 (odd), [6,7,4] OR=7 (odd) → 2.
First case all numbers are even, so every window has even OR → 0. Second case windows: [1,2,4] contains odd → odd OR, [2,4,6] all even → even OR → answer 1.

solution.approach: The OR of a subarray is odd if and only if the subarray contains at least one odd element, because the least‑significant bit of the OR equals the OR of the least‑significant bits of the elements. Therefore we only need to count windows of size K that contain at least one odd number. The total number of windows is N‑K+1. We subtract the number of windows that consist solely of even numbers. To obtain the latter, scan the array and find maximal consecutive segments of even numbers; a segment of length L contributes max(0, L‑K+1) all‑even windows. The answer is total‑allEven. This is O(N) per test case and O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
